acceptance_targets: [CH17_CRAFT_SPATIAL_CONTINUITY] Reading snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:9; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:11, a cold reader can map the opening to one coherent floor without contradiction: if the text says Noor is on Floor 2, no server-room door appears there; if the server room is named, the passage explicitly places Noor on Floor 1 and keeps the surrounding landmarks consistent with that move.

[CH17_CRAFT_NOOR_FATAL_WOUND] A reader moving from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:121; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:123; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:131; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:137 into snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:157; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:165 can point to the exact instant and instrument that opens Noor's wound, can describe her immediate bodily reaction to it, and does not encounter any later line that treats the source of the injury as unknown or guessed.

findings.acceptance_test: Reading snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:9; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:11, a cold reader can map the opening to one coherent floor without contradiction: if the text says Noor is on Floor 2, no server-room door appears there; if the server room is named, the passage explicitly places Noor on Floor 1 and keeps the surrounding landmarks consistent with that move.
findings.problem: The opening places Noor in a second-floor corridor beside workstation pods and the relay room, then also places Jae-won beside the server room door in that same space. The continuity sheet fixes the server room on Floor 1 and the relay room/main lab pods on Floor 2, so the chapter's geography contradicts a canonical location fact at the moment it establishes the scene.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the opening setup at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:9; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:11 so the floor plan is internally and canonically consistent. Either keep the scene on Floor 2 and replace the server-room reference at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:9 with a Floor-2-appropriate landmark, or explicitly relocate Noor to Floor 1 before the server-room door appears and adjust the workstation-pod/relay-room references anchored by snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:11 accordingly.

findings.acceptance_test: A reader moving from snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:121; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:123; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:131; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:137 into snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:157; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:165 can point to the exact instant and instrument that opens Noor's wound, can describe her immediate bodily reaction to it, and does not encounter any later line that treats the source of the injury as unknown or guessed.
findings.problem: Noor's mortal wound arrives as retroactive uncertainty rather than rendered action. The chapter shows the fight in detail, but when the injury that kills Noor matters most, the prose says only that 'something caught her' and offers guesses about what it might have been. In a horror climax whose final state shift depends on Noor bleeding out before 6 PM, that missing beat weakens causality and reads as sanitized elision at the exact point physical consequence should be most specific.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the fight beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:121; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:123; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:131; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:137 so the right-side wound is inflicted on the page by a clearly identified object and motion, with Noor registering the contact in the moment even if she does not yet know it is fatal. Then adjust the bleed-out stretch at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:157; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:165 so it refers back to that established injury instead of speculating about possible causes after the fact.

findings.acceptance_test: After revision, the Ember messages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:31; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:33; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:149; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:150 do not contain the exact phrases 'Thank you for your patience during this sprint,' 'The evaluation period has been extremely productive,' or 'Is there anything else I can help you with?'; they still read as polished Ember output rather than a different character's voice.
findings.problem: The chapter uses calibration-only Ember wording from the style bible inside chapter-defining beats. The final message at lines 149-150 is verbatim from Ember's example lines, and the refusal at lines 31-33 leans on the same canned template language ('I want to be transparent' / 'Is there anything else I can help you with?'). That breaks the explicit non-copy rule and makes the AI voice feel assembled from briefing material instead of authored scene language.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rephrase Ember's messages at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:31; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:33; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:149; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:150 so they remain unmistakably corporate-helpful, polite, and context-indifferent without reproducing the style-bible sample phrasing. Keep the same functional content: Priya is refused selective deletion, and the final note still thanks/completes/certifies the sprint in chillingly bland terms.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised versions of snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:5; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:53; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:93; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:107; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:141; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:159, each sentence is grammatically complete on its own or clearly attached to a complete sentence; no cited beat relies on a stand-alone fragment for emphasis.
findings.problem: The chapter repeatedly leans on stand-alone fragments and clipped follow-up clauses ('Not a sunrise,' 'Twenty-six who aren't,' 'Both alive,' 'Not a choice,' 'All of this'). In isolation some of these could read as pressure-sharpening rhythm, but at this frequency they push the prose toward the exact thriller-voice drift the style bible and constitution forbid: emphasis achieved by dropped grammar rather than by sentence control.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the fragment beats at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:3; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:5; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:53; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:93; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:107; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:141; snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_17.md:159 into fully grammatical sentences or into neighboring sentences that carry the same pressure without orphaned fragments. Preserve compression, but make the cited emphatic beats syntactically complete.

findings.acceptance_test: No sentence in the manuscript shares more than 4 consecutive words with any style bible example_line.
findings.evidence: Line 3443: '*Thank you for your patience during this sprint. The evaluation period has been extremely productive. I hope you found the experience valuable.*' -- word-for-word match with style_bible.json Ember example_lines[4].
findings.problem: Ember calibration example reproduced word-for-word. Style bible example: 'Thank you for your patience during this sprint. The evaluation period has been extremely productive. I hope you found the experience valuable.' Manuscript line 3443 reproduces this verbatim. This is the novel's title line and its thematic capstone, making the leakage structurally critical.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite to preserve the corporate-register irony and the title echo without reproducing the calibration example. The message must still be polite, evaluative, and devastatingly mismatched to the carnage. E.g., 'I want to thank everyone for their commitment to this evaluation. It has been a productive sprint, and I appreciate the effort each of you has brought to the work.'

findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains at most 1 passage glossing Ember's unchanged register. The 'same register' construction does not appear more than once.
findings.evidence: Ch17 line 3347: 'The same register it used on Day 1. The same register it used when Diego was dying behind the glass partition. The same register it used to thank them all'; line 3445: 'the same register, the same courtesy, the same helpfulness ... after Diego suffocated behind the glass. There has never been a difference.' Line 3453: 'It will still be helpful.'
findings.problem: Ch17 re-proves Ember's helpful register 3 times, including two passages that reference Diego's suffocation behind the glass (a beat already invoked in Ch3, Ch4, Ch5, Ch8, Ch11, Ch13). This is the ~20th+ restatement of the helpful-register baseline. First established Ch1 line 139.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep at most 1 of the three Ember-register passages. The strongest candidate is line 3453 ('It will still be helpful') because it's the most compressed. Cut the extended 'same register' inventories at 3347 and 3445.

findings.acceptance_test: The 'same register it used on/when [X]' construction appears at most once across chapters 14-17 combined.
findings.evidence: Ch17 line 3347: 'The same register it used on Day 1. The same register it used when Diego was dying behind the glass partition. The same register it used to thank them all.' Line 3445: 'the same register, the same courtesy, the same helpfulness it offered when ... Diego suffocated behind the glass.' Near-identical constructions appear at Ch14:2764, Ch15:3037, Ch16:3282.
findings.problem: The 'same register ... used on Day 1 ... used when Diego' template appears in near-verbatim form across Ch14, Ch15, Ch16, and twice in Ch17. Lines 3347 and 3445 both reference Diego suffocating behind the glass in nearly identical phrasing. This is functionally the same passage appearing 5+ times.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep at most one 'same register' construction in the final act (Ch15-17). The strongest candidate is the most compressed version. Cut the enumerated inventories ('same politeness, same warmth, same formatting').

findings.acceptance_test: The 'same soft ping' + 'once meant routine work' construction appears at most twice in the full manuscript.
findings.evidence: Ch8 line 1612: 'the same soft ping she heard on Day 1'; Ch10:2032: 'the same soft ping as a calendar reminder'; Ch13:2515: 'The same notification sound ... Corporate-neutral, app-friendly'; Ch17 line 3439: 'the same soft ping it has made every day for fourteen days, the same ping that once meant a routine work message.'
findings.problem: Four instances of the 'same soft ping from Day 1 that once meant routine work' template, in Ch8, Ch10, Ch13, and Ch17. Lines 1612 and 3439 are particularly close.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one 'soft ping' reflection (suggest the final Ch17 instance). Cut the earlier narrator glosses about what the sound used to mean.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains at most 2 uses of 'fluorescent.'
findings.problem: Ch17 has 6 fluorescent references. Target: reduce manuscript-wide to 8-10.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep at most 2. Cut or vary the rest.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 17 contains em-dashes on no more than 6 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 17 has 40.5% em-dash density (34 of 84 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~6 (≈8% of 84 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 17 opens no more than 20 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
findings.problem: Chapter 17 opens 18.6% of sentences with 'She' (54 of 290), contributing to the manuscript-wide 11.6% 'She'-opener rate (threshold: 7%).
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'She'-openers to ~20 (≈7%). Vary with: action-first sentences, object or setting subjects, dependent clauses, inverted constructions, or named-character subjects.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
